sections.paragraph:
Resh, the twentieth Hebrew letter, as a numeral, i.q. 200. The name רֵישׁ, i.q. Chald. רֵאשׁ and Heb. רֹאשׁ, denotes the head, and refers to the form of this letter in the Phœnician alphabet (), from which, with the head turned back, comes the figure of the Greek Ῥῶ.

This letter is cognate-

(1) as being the hardest of the liquids, with the other liquids, and sometimes with ל and נ, see pp. 421, A , and 523, A .

(2) as being partly pronounced in the throat with the guttural ע, p. 598, A .

(3) it is sometimes interchanged with the sibilants, especially with ז, compare בָּרַק and בָּזַק to emit rays, חָרַם, חָסַם, Arab. خرم and خزم; also رمّ and زم to muzzle, فخر and فخز to be proud, and others. See also the paronomasia in the words הָרֹון and חָזֹון Eze 7:13.

It is also to be observed that sometimes a single letter, with ר inserted before it, is used instead of a double letter; and this takes place especially in the Aramean and the later Hebrew, as כִּסֵא, Ch. כָּרְסֵא a throne; דַּמֶּשֶׂק in Chron. דַּרְמֶשֶׂק Damascus; כִּרְבֵּל quadrilit., formed from Piel כִּבֵּל to bind; also סַרְבֵּל, כִּרְסֵם, קַרְדֹּם; similarly, too, we must explain שַׁרְבִּיט a sceptre, i.q. שֵׁבֶט; Ch. גַּרְמִידָא cubit, i.q. גֹּמֶד, formed from שַׁבִּיט, גַּמִּיד, although these forms are no longer found.
